content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 10, 1993

Paul B. Webb
Land Agent Officer
Department of Parks and Recreation
Southern Service Center
8885 Rio San Diego Drive, Suite 270
San Diego, California 92108

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-382

Dear Mr. Webb:

This letter is in response to your request for advice
concerning your duties and responsibilities under the provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 We do not provide
advice concerning provisions of law other than the Act. You may
wish to consult with the Attorney General's Office regarding other
provisions of law which may be applicable to you such as the
doctrine of incompatible offices.

QUESTION

Do the "revolving door" provisions of the Act apply to you as
an employee of the State Department of Parks and Recreation with
respect to the granting of easements, permits or other actions
related to a project regulated by your former employer, the
California Coastal Commission?

CONCLUSION

The "revolving door" provisions of the Act under Sections
87400 to 87405 are not applicable where an employee of a state
agency transfers to another state agency.

_________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Sections,
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-382
Page 2


FACTS

You are a former employee of the California Coastal
Commission. You served as a Coastal Program Analyst I and a
Coastal Program Analyst II. Your service there ended on
July 1, 1993, when you accepted a position as a State Park Land
Officer with the California Department of Parks and Recreation.

As an employee of the California Coastal Commission, your
duties involved regulatory planning for development within the
coastal zone and review of long range planning documents submitted
by local government agencies. You were the lead analyst for the
Batiquitos Lagoon Enhancement Project, a large restoration project
for a coastal lagoon in northern San Diego County.

At the Department of Parks and Recreation, your duties
include issuance of permits for non-park uses of state park lands,
preparation of easement documents, right-of-way permits and
operating agreements for private and public uses of park lands,
and the negotiation and management of leases of state park lands.

The Department of Parks and Recreation owns a park unit
located on the shoreline at Batiquitos Lagoon. Although the
department is not a party to the memorandum of understanding that
was executed to allow for the restoration plan, project proponents
must obtain easements, right-of way permits, and temporary use
permits from the department.

At the present time, you have been approached by a consultant
for the City of Carlsbad and the Port of Los Angeles regarding
permits and or easements for these construction activities
associated with this project.

The California Coastal Commission and the California
Department of Parks and Recreation are separate state agencies.

ANALYSIS

The Act places certain restrictions upon "state administra-
tive officials" who leave their position with the state. "State
administrative official" means every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of his or
her official responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-
judicial or other proceeding in other than a purely clerical,
secretarial or ministerial capacity. (Section 87400(b).)

As an employee of the California Coastal Commission you
served as a state administrative official subject to the
provisions of the Act. (Id.)

Sections 87401 and 87402 provide:

No former state administrative official, after
the termination of his or her employment or term of

Our File No. A-93-382
Page 3


     office, shall for compensation act as agent or at-
torney for, or otherwise represent, any other
person (other than the State of California) before
any court or state administrative agency or any
officer or employee thereof by making any formal or
informal appearance, or by making any oral or writ-
ten communication with the intent to influence, in
connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding if both of the following apply:

     (a)  The State of California is a party or has
a direct and substantial interest.

     (b)  The proceeding is one in which the former
state administrative official participated.

                    Section 87401.  (Emphasis added.)

     No former state administrative official, after
the termination of his or her employment or term of
office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel,
consult or assist in representing any other person
(except the State of California) in any proceeding
in which the official would be prohibited from ap-
pearing under Section 87401.

                    Section 87402.  (Emphasis added.)

     Thus, once you leave your state employment, Sections 87401
and 87402 prohibit you from representing, aiding, advising,
counseling, consulting, or assisting in representing any person,
for compensation, in connection with any proceeding in which you
participated as an employee of the California Coastal Commission.
However, since Sections 87401 and 87402 provide for a specific
exclusion for persons representing the State of California, these
provisions are not applicable where an employee of one state
agency accepts employment with another state agency.2

     The California Coastal Commission and the California Parks
and Recreation Department are separate state agencies within the
State of California.  Thus, you will continue to represent the
State of California, and the exclusions of Section 87401 and 87402
apply.3  Therefore, you are not prevented from representing the

2    A "state agency" means ever state office, department, division,
bureau, board and commission, and the Legislature.  (Section
82049.)

3    Please note, we have also advised that the "revolving door"
provisions of Sections 87400-87405 do not prevent a former state
administrative official who has left his or her state employment
from contracting with other state agencies.  (McWhirk Advice
Letter, No. A-89-392; Walsh Advice Letter No. A-90-281.)

Our File No. A-93-382
Page 4


California Parks and Recreation, whether or not your work involves
matters on which you worked as an employee of the California
Coastal Commission.

This advice is limited to application of the Act. We suggest
that you contact the Attorney General's office regarding other
provisions of law which may apply, such as the doctrine of
incompatible offices.

Please note that since Sections 87406 and 87407 do not appear
to be applicable to your facts, we have not addressed post-
government employment restrictions covered under those sections of
the Act. However, we have enclosed general information regarding
these laws (copy enclosed). Should you need specific guidance
with respect to a specific set of facts under these laws, you
should seek further written assistance from the Commission.

Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure
